title: 1987 World Snooker Championship
plaintext:
The 1987 World Snooker Championship (also referred to as the 1987 Embassy World Snooker Championship for the purpose of sponsorship) was a professional snooker tournament that took place between 18 April and 4 May 1987 at the Crucible Theatre in Sheffield, England. It was the sixth and final ranking event of the 1986–87 snooker season. The championship was the 1987 edition of the World Snooker Championship, first held in 1927, and had 32 participants. The highest ranked 16 players were awarded a place in the first round draw, whilst a pre-tournament qualification event for 104 professionals was held between 26 March and 4 April at the Preston Guild Hall for the remaining places. The tournament was sponsored by cigarette manufacturer Embassy and had a prize fund of £400,000 with the winner receiving £80,000.
Since his 1986 victory, Joe Johnson had experienced a disappointing season leading up to the 1987 Championship, and bookmakers considered it unlikely that he would retain the title. Johnson did reach the final, a rematch of the previous year's final against Steve Davis. Davis won his fourth championship by defeating Johnson 18 frames to 14.  A total of 18 century breaks were made during the tournament, the highest of which was 127 made by Davis in first frame of the final. Stephen Hendry, aged 18, became the youngest player to win a match in the tournament's history since it moved to the Crucible in 1977, whilst it was the last time that six-times champion Ray Reardon appeared.


== Overview ==
The World Snooker Championship is a professional tournament and the official world championship of the game of snooker. Founded in the late 19th century by British Army soldiers stationed in India, the sport was popular in the British Isles. However, in the modern era it has become increasingly popular worldwide, especially in East and Southeast Asian nations such as China, Hong Kong and Thailand.
The 1987 championship featured 32 professional players competing in one-on-one snooker matches in a single elimination format, each played over several frames. The 32 competitors in the main tournament were selected using a combination of the top players in the world snooker rankings and a pre-tournament qualification stage. The tournament was promoted by WPBSA Promotions, a subsidiary of professional snooker governing body the World Professional Billiards and Snooker Association (WPBSA). Joe Davis won the first World Championship in 1927, the final match being held in Camkin's Hall, Birmingham, England. Since 1977, the event has been held in the Crucible Theatre in Sheffield, England.


=== Format ===
The championship was held from 18 April to 4 May 1987 at the Crucible, the 11th time that the tournament was held at the venue. It was the last ranking event of the 1986–87 snooker season on the World Snooker Tour. There were a total of 120 entrants from the tour, and the competition's main draw had 32 participants. A four-round knockout qualifying competition was held at Preston Guild Hall from 26 March to 4 April which produced the 16 qualifying players who progressed into the main draw to play the top 16 seeds.
The top 16 players in the latest world rankings automatically qualified for the main draw as seeded players. As defending champion, Joe Johnson was seeded first for the event; the remaining 15 seeds were allocated based on the players' world ranking positions. Matches in the first round of the main draw were played as best-of-19-frames, meaning 10 frames were required to win the match. The number of frames needed to win a match increased to 13 in the second round and quarter-finals, and 16 in the semi-finals; the final match was played as best-of-35-frames.


=== Prize fund ===
The event featured a prize fund of £400,000 with the winner receiving £80,000. The breakdown of prize money for the event is shown below:


== Tournament summary ==
The defending champion, Joe Johnson, had failed to reach as far as the quarter-finals of a major tournament in the 1986–87 snooker season after winning the 1986 World Championship. This record was described by The Sydney Morning Herald's Les Wheeler as "disappointing" and by Clive Everton as a "poor" season. Sydney Friskin of The Times reported that Johnson prepared for the Championship by practising diligently, and that the cyst on his back that previously troubled him had been removed. Johnson started the event as a bookmakers' outsider, priced at 66–1 against winning the tournament.
Leading up to the event, Neal Foulds had been the most successful player of the season, having gained the most ranking points during the season, ahead of Steve Davis. Twelve days before the start of the tournament, the two-times world champion Alex Higgins was fined £12,000 and given a six-month ban from tournaments by the WPBSA. The ban started on 5 May, the day after the final of the 1987 World Snooker Championship. The penalties resulted from a number of incidents, the most serious of which was headbutting Paul Hatherall, a WPBSA tournament director, at the 1986 UK Championship.


=== Qualifying ===

There were four rounds of qualifying, with higher ranked players seeded into the later rounds, and 104 entrants which took place at the Preston Guild Hall between 25 March and 4 April 1987. The 16 winners in the fourth round progressed to play the tournament's top 16 seeds at the Crucible. All qualifying matches were best-of-19 frames held over two sessions. There were 24 matches scheduled in the first qualifying round, but Frank Jonik, Eddie McLaughlin, Sakchai Sim Ngam and Omprakesh Agrawal all withdrew, meaning that their opponents received walkovers. The 11-time pool world champion, Jim Rempe, made a break of 104 in defeating Martin Smith 10–9. Veteran professional Bernard Bennett suffered the only whitewash of the first round, failing to win a frame against Billy Kelly.
In the second round, there were 32 matches. The youngest player in the competition, Stephen Hendry, made a break of 108 during his 10–7 defeat of Mike Darrington. Eight-times champion Fred Davis lost 5–10 to Ken Owers. Another former champion, John Spencer, who had won the title three times, eliminated Roger Bales 10–3. Trailing after the first session of his qualifying match 3–5, Jimmy van Rensberg was taken to hospital with a suspected heart attack. However, he was later discharged and went on to win 10–6.
The third round consisted of 16 matches between winners from the second round. Hendry led 8–1 against Rempe at the end of their first session, with Rempe winning three consecutive frames in their second session before Hendry won the match 10–4. Gino Rigitano conceded the 11th frame of his match against Steve Newbury when there were still enough balls on the table for him to win, and when 4–9 down decided not to play the next frame, thereby losing 4–10. There were two 10–0 whitewashes: by Jon Wright over Mark Wildman and by Tony Jones over van Rensberg.
The fourth round also featured 16 matches, with 16 players seeded into the round each meeting one of the third round winners. For the first time since turning professional, John Spencer failed to qualify for the event, as he was defeated 5–10 by Barry West. Bill Werbeniuk and Eddie Charlton also failed to qualify for the competition for the first time. Werbeniuk lost 8–10 to Mark Bennett and Charlton was defeated 4–10 by Warren King. The only match to go to a deciding frame in round four was John Virgo's 10–9 win over Tony Jones. Dene O'Kane scored five century breaks across his three matches, a new record, including a 132. He received £2,000 for this break, the highest during qualifying.


=== First round ===

The first round of the main tournament was held from 18 to 23 April with matches played as the best-of-19 frames over two sessions. Defending champion Joe Johnson played Eugene Hughes, with the match going to a deciding frame and Johnson winning 10–9. Steve Davis was 7–1 ahead of Warren King at the end of their first session, but King then won six of the next seven frames to reduce Davis's lead to one frame at 8–7. Davis narrowly won frame 16 with a break of 63, then completed a 10–7 victory in frame 17, where he successfully gained enough foul points from King, despite requiring snookers.
Murdo MacLeod defeated Rex Williams 10–5, despite Williams making the highest break of the first round, a 112. The win made MacLeod the first Scottish player to secure a victory at the Crucible Theatre. Stephen Hendry met veteran player Willie Thorne, led 5–4 at the end of their first session, and then took the first four frames in the second, before Thorne won three in a row. Hendry took the 17th frame to achieve a 10–7 win. At age 18 years and 97 days, Hendry became the youngest-ever player to win a World Championship match at the Crucible. Steve Longworth led 5–4 after the first session of his match, and won five successive frames to defeat Kirk Stevens 10–5. Terry Griffiths also progressed from a 5–4 interval lead to a 10–4 win, against Jim Wych. Alex Higgins, due to start a six-month ban after the Championship, eliminated first-year professional Jon Wright 10–6.
Jimmy White led Dean Reynolds 5–4 at the end of their first session. Reynolds won the first two frames of the second session both on respotted blacks, before White went on to win 10–8. From 7–8, White made breaks of 70, 75 and 59 to win the three frames he needed to progress. O'Kane, 39th in the rankings, won nine frames in a row against second-ranked player Cliff Thorburn to win 10–5. Thorburn's cue tip split during the sixth frame and was not replaced until after the end of the first session. He was unable to win a frame after the tip was broken. Six-time champion Ray Reardon defeated debutant Barry West 10–5. Fourth seed Tony Knowles lost 6–10 to Mike Hallett, having led 6–5, and 11th seed Tony Meo lost 8–10 to John Parrott.
Silvino Francisco and John Campbell played only eight of their scheduled nine frames in the first session, due to slow play, with Francisco leading 5–3 when they started the following session. Francisco won five consecutive frames at the start of the second session to complete a 10–3 win. Doug Mountjoy led David Taylor 6–3 after their first session, and won 10–6. The 1985 champion Dennis Taylor led Bennett 8–1 before winning 10–4. Neal Foulds led John Virgo 7–2 after their first session and won 10–4.


=== Second round ===
The second round was played from 23 to 27 April with matches as the best-of-25 frames held over three sessions. Johnson led MacLeod 6–2 and 10–6 after the first two sessions, winning 13–7. Hendry and Longworth were level at 4–4 after their first session, with Hendry then opening up a 7–4 lead with breaks of 96, 54 and 89, and finishing the second session 10–6 ahead after winning the 16th frame on a re-spotted black. Later trailing by six frames, Longworth won the 19th with a 103 break, before Hendry completed a 13–7 victory. Francisco and Hallett were at 8–8 after their second session before Hallett won three frames in a row, with breaks of 84, 47, 35 and 93. He also won the 20th frame, after requiring foul shots from Francisco, to lead 12–8, and clinched victory at 13–9 to reach his first World Championship quarter-final. Foulds led Taylor 5–3 after their first session, and won the match 13–10.
O'Kane eliminated another top-16 player with a victory over Mountjoy. Mountjoy led 3–0, but O'Kane then won the next six frames, and won 13–5. White made a break of 114 in his match against Parrott, the highest in that year's championship to that point, and won 13–11. Five of the frames had been decided on the black, with White winning four of them. Higgins led Griffiths 6–3 after the first session but Griffiths won four frames in a row and the match was tied at 8–8. Griffiths went on to defeat Higgins 13–10. Six-times winner of the event Reardon led 3–1 but lost 12 of the next 13 frames as he was eliminated 4–13 by Davis. After the tournament, Reardon dropped out of the top 16 in the end-of-season world rankings, moving down from 15 to 38.


=== Quarter-finals ===

The quarter-final matches were played over three sessions, in best-of-25 frames, on 28 and 29 April. Johnson led Hendry 8–1 and 12–8, before Hendry won four frames in a row to take the match to a deciding frame at 12–12. In the final frame, after Hendry failed to pot a red ball, Johnson made a break of 46 to take the frame and match 13–12. Hallett compiled two century breaks in the first session of his match against Foulds, who also constructed a century break, with their first session ending at 4–4. Foulds then opened up a lead, leading 10–6 and 12–7 before winning at 13–9.
Davis was 4–3 ahead of Griffiths after their first session, and 10–5 ahead by the end of the second. He wrapped up a 13–5 win and with breaks of 62, 86 and 51 in three frames in the final session. It was the fifth time that Davis had defeated Griffiths at the World Championship, and the largest winning margin of those encounters. White won all eight frames in the first session in his match against O'Kane, and after winning the first frame of the second session, led 9–0. O'Kane then won five consecutive frames, before White took four of the following five frames to win 13–6.


=== Semi-finals ===
The semi-final matches were played as the best-of-31 frames, held over four sessions, from 30 April to 2 May. Johnson met Foulds in the first semi-final. The pair were tied at 3–3, but Foulds missed a pot on the black ball in frame seven allowing Johnson to take a one-frame lead after the first session. In frame eight, Foulds made a break of 48 to win the frame, and won frame nine, despite requiring foul shots. Johnson made breaks of 47 in each of the next two frames to lead 6–5. Foulds took the next before Johnson, with his fourth break of 47 in four frames, took the lead again. Foulds won the last frame of the session with a break of 45 to leave them all square at 7–7 after two sessions, and made a break of 114 in the 15th frame to go one ahead before Johnson won seven frames in a row to lead 14–8. After this, Foulds won frame 23, but Johnson won the next two frames to win the match 16–9 to reach his second final.
Davis and White had been level at 4–4 after their first session, with Davis winning the first four of their second session to lead 8–4 and finishing that session 9–6 ahead. In the first frame of the third session, White was on course to make a maximum break, having potted ten reds and nine blacks, but missed the tenth black. After this, Davis required White to make foul shots in order to gain the necessary penalty points from them for Davis to win the frame. Aided by a fluked black, and by a free ball following a foul by White, Davis eventually won the frame by one point. John Hennessey in Pot Black magazine wrote that "at that moment White lost the chance of claiming his first world title," adding that White's father later said that losing the frame affected White badly during the following three. However, White later compiled a 119 break, the new highest in the competition, overtaking his earlier 114 in the second round, and ended the third session 9–13 behind. White took the first frame of the fourth session, but lost three of the following four frame as Davis won the match 16–11, concluding with a 74 break in the 27th frame.


=== Final ===

The final was played as a best-of-35 frames match held over four sessions between Davis and Johnson on 3 and 4 May 1987. It was the first time that the same two players had met in the final at the Crucible for the second year in a row. The last time that two players had met in consecutive finals at the World Championship was when Fred Davis and John Pulman had both reached the final in 1955 and 1956, played at the Tower Circus, Blackpool. The next time it would happen was when Hendry and White met in three consecutive finals between 1992 and 1994. This was also the first time that the final had been contested by the top two seeds of the tournament. The final was refereed by Len Ganley, the second time he had taken charge of the World Championship final.
In frame one, Davis compiled a 127 break, which remained the highest break of that year's tournament. Johnson responded winning three frames in a row followed by Davis taking two to level at 3-3. Johnson was 4–3 ahead at the end of the first session. Davis added three successive frames to lead 6–4 at the start of the second session, then Johnson won the 11th frame with a break of 101 before falling three behind as Davis won the next two frames. Johnson was behind in the 14th frame but won it with a break of 73. The last two frames of the session were both won with the aid of fluked reds: the 15th frame by Davis, and the last of the day by Johnson, the day finishing with Davis leading 9–7. On the second day, Johnson won the first frame of the third session to reduce Davis's lead to one frame. Davis then took four consecutive frames to lead 13–8, but missed the last red ball when on a break of 52. Johnson then cleared up to the black, which Davis would have required to level the scores in the frame. However, Davis left the black in a position that it could be potted from, and Johnson won the frame. Davis won the next frame, to lead 14–9 at the end of the third session.
Johnson made a break of 52 in the first frame of the fourth session, but failed on an attempt to pot a red, which gave Davis an opportunity. Davis then made a break of 35, but left an easy green ball for Johnson, who cleared to the pink ball to win. In the next frame, Johnson made a break of 62, and then Davis attempted a clearance, but missed the yellow ball. Johnson potted the yellow from distance and cleared to the blue, with Davis then conceding the frame. Johnson led 50–0 in the next frame, and with both players making a number of errors Davis left him an easy brown that allowed Johnson in to win his fourth consecutive frame to move to one behind at 13–14. Davis had breaks of 64 and 40 to lead 15–13, a break of 73 to help make it 16–13, and 17–13 winning frame 30, decided on the final colours. Johnson won another frame, with a break of 67, before Davis clinched victory with a break of 73 to make it 18–14, achieving his fourth World Championship title. After the match, Davis said: "Winning this is better than 1981 because I've experienced getting beat in the final and its horrible."


== Aftermath ==
During the tournament, there were reports that Foulds and WPBSA chairman Williams were taking beta blockers. These were banned under International Olympic Committee rules, but not prohibited in snooker. Colin Moynihan, a Member of the British Parliament, called for Williams to resign and any players using beta blockers to withdraw from competing. In November 1987, the WPBSA was suspended from using the Sports Council's drug-testing facilities until the use of beta blockers was banned from the sport. Moynihan wrote to Williams supporting the ban that had been proposed by the Sports Council's Drug Advisory Group. Williams resigned as WPBSA chairman in November 1987, having received criticism over the drug testing issue as well as over his personal business connections with promoters Barry Hearn and Frank Warren. The WPBSA voted to ban the use of beta blockers, other than cardioselective types, in January 1988, with the ban to come into effect from the start of the 1988–89 snooker season.

== Century breaks ==
There were 18 century breaks at the championship. The highest was a 127 made by Steve Davis in the first frame of the final. This was the lowest world championship high break since the event moved to the Crucible Theatre in 1977. The highest break in 1977 was 135 by John Spencer, which was the lowest to be the highest break at the world championship until 1986, when Davis's 134 was the highest.

The '''1987 World Snooker Championship''' (also referred to as the '''1987 Embassy World Snooker Championship''' for the purpose of sponsorship) was a professional [[snooker]] tournament that took place between 18 April and 4 May 1987 at the [[Crucible Theatre]] in [[Sheffield]], England. It was the sixth and final [[Snooker world rankings|ranking]] event of the [[1986–87 snooker season]]. The championship was the 1987 edition of the [[World Snooker Championship]], first held in [[1927 World Snooker Championship|1927]], and had 32 participants. The highest ranked 16 players were awarded a place in the first round draw, whilst a pre-tournament qualification event for 104 professionals was held between 26 March and 4 April at the [[Preston Guild Hall]] for the remaining places. The tournament was sponsored by cigarette manufacturer [[Embassy (cigarette)|Embassy]] and had a prize fund of £400,000 with the winner receiving £80,000.

Since his [[1986 World Snooker Championship|1986 victory]], [[Joe Johnson (snooker player)|Joe Johnson]] had experienced a disappointing season leading up to the 1987 Championship, and [[bookmaker]]s considered it unlikely that he would retain the title. Johnson did reach the final, a rematch of the previous year's final against [[Steve Davis]]. Davis won his fourth championship by defeating Johnson 18 {{cuegloss|frames}} to 14.  A total of 18 [[century break]]s were made during the tournament, the highest of which was 127 made by Davis in first frame of the final. [[Stephen Hendry]], aged 18, became the youngest player to win a match in the tournament's history since it moved to the Crucible in 1977, whilst it was the last time that six-times champion [[Ray Reardon]] appeared.

=== Format ===
The championship was held from 18 April to 4 May 1987 at the Crucible, the 11th time that the tournament was held at the venue.<ref name="DOWNER19">{{cite book|last=Downer|first=Chris|title=Crucible Almanac |chapter=1987|year=2019 |location=Bournemouth |pages=4–25}}</ref> It was the last ranking event of the [[1986–87 snooker season]] on the [[World Snooker Tour]].<ref name="87HALE">{{cite book |last=Hale |first=Janice |date=1987 |title=Rothmans Snooker Yearbook 1987–88  |location=Aylesbury |publisher=Queen Anne Press |isbn=0-356-14690-1 |pages=13–14 }}</ref> There were a total of 120 entrants from the tour, and the competition's main draw had 32 participants.<ref name="DOWNER19" /> A four-round knockout qualifying competition was held at [[Preston Guild Hall]] from 26 March to 4 April which produced the 16 qualifying players who progressed into the main draw to play the top 16 [[seed (sports)|seeds]].<ref name="SCENEQ" />

The top 16 players in the latest [[Snooker world rankings 1986/1987|world rankings]] automatically qualified for the main draw as seeded players.{{efn|If the defending champion was ranked outside the top 16 in the world rankings as an automatic qualifier.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Caulfield |first=David |title=On This Day in Crucible History: May 4 – Steve Davis Reclaims Crown |website=snookerhq.com |publisher=SnookerHQ |date=4 May 2019 |access-date=25 May 2020 |url=https://snookerhq.com/2019/05/04/on-this-day-in-crucible-history-may-4-steve-davis-reclaims-crown/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200503123828/https://snookerhq.com/2019/05/04/on-this-day-in-crucible-history-may-4-steve-davis-reclaims-crown/ |archive-date=3 May 2020 |url-status=live}}</ref>}} As defending champion, [[Joe Johnson (snooker player)|Joe Johnson]] was seeded first for the event; the remaining 15 seeds were allocated based on the players' world ranking positions.<ref name=snookerscene/> Matches in the first round of the main draw were played as best-of-19-frames, meaning 10 frames were required to win the match. The number of frames needed to win a match increased to 13 in the second round and quarter-finals, and 16 in the semi-finals; the final match was played as best-of-35-frames.<ref name="Ftsx0">{{cite book |last=Hale |first=Janice |date=1991 |title=Rothmans Snooker Yearbook 1991–92 |location=Aylesbury |publisher=Queen Anne Press |isbn=0-356-19747-6 |pages=29, 103, 356}}</ref><ref name="sC611">{{cite web |title=1987 World Championships Results Grid |url=http://snookerdatabase.co.uk/EventResultGrid.aspx?EventKey=25 |website=snookerdatabase.co.uk |publisher=Snooker Database |access-date=28 October 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110827123257/http://snookerdatabase.co.uk/EventResultGrid.aspx?EventKey=25 |archive-date=27 August 2011 |url-status=live}}</ref>

== Tournament summary ==
The defending champion, Joe Johnson, had failed to reach as far as the quarter-finals of a major tournament in the 1986–87 snooker season after winning the [[1986 World Snooker Championship|1986 World Championship]].<ref name="BAIZE">{{cite book |last1=Williams |first1=Luke |last2=Gadsby |first2=Paul |date=2005 |title=Masters of the Baize |location=Edinburgh |publisher=Mainstream |isbn=1-84018-872-3 |pages=140–141}}</ref> This record was described by ''[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]''{{'s}} Les Wheeler as "disappointing" and by [[Clive Everton]] as a "poor" season.<ref name="z6zQf">{{cite news|last=Wheeler|first=Les|date=16 April 1987|title=King to meet the world no 1|page=38|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|location=Australia}}</ref><ref name="FARCE">{{cite book |last=Everton |first=Clive |date=2012 |title=Black farce and cue ball wizards |publisher=Mainstream |location=Edinburgh |isbn=978-1-78057-568-1 |page=104}}</ref> Sydney Friskin of ''[[The Times]]'' reported that Johnson prepared for the Championship by practising diligently, and that the [[cyst]] on his back that previously troubled him had been removed.<ref name="3Alg4">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Memories of the Crucible that haunt Davis |work=The Times |location=London |date=17 April 1987 |page=20}}</ref> Johnson started the event as a [[bookmaker]]s' outsider, [[fixed-odds betting|priced]] at 66–1 against winning the tournament.<ref name="alTkY">{{cite book |editor=Terry Smith |date=1987 |title=Benson and Hedges Snooker Year |location=Aylesbury |publisher=Pelham Books |isbn=0-7207-1797-3 |pages=76–80 |edition=Fourth}}</ref>

Leading up to the event, [[Neal Foulds]] had been the most successful player of the season, having gained the most ranking points during the season, ahead of [[Steve Davis]].<ref name="z6zQf" /> Twelve days before the start of the tournament, the two-times world champion [[Alex Higgins]] was fined £12,000 and given a six-month ban from tournaments by the WPBSA. The ban started on 5 May, the day after the final of the 1987 World Snooker Championship. The penalties resulted from a number of incidents, the most serious of which was headbutting Paul Hatherall, a WPBSA tournament director, at the [[1986 UK Championship]].<ref name="TIMES0704">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Higgins banned for six months and fined £12,000 |work=The Times |location=London |date=7 April 1987 |page=44}}</ref>

===Qualifying===
[[File:Preston Guild Hall 1.jpg|thumb|alt=An image of the Preston Guild Hall|The [[Preston Guild Hall]] hosted qualifying for the event.]]
There were four rounds of qualifying, with higher ranked players seeded into the later rounds, and 104 entrants which took place at the [[Preston Guild Hall]] between 25 March and 4 April 1987. The 16 winners in the fourth round progressed to play the tournament's top 16 seeds at the Crucible. All qualifying matches were best-of-19 frames held over two {{cuegloss|sessions}}.<ref name="Ftsx0" /> There were 24 matches scheduled in the first qualifying round, but [[Frank Jonik]], Eddie McLaughlin, [[Sakchai Sim Ngam]] and [[Omprakesh Agrawal]] all withdrew, meaning that their opponents received [[walkover]]s.<ref name="DOWNERQ">{{cite book|last=Downer|first=Chris|title=Crucible Almanac |chapter=1987 qualifying rounds|year=2019|pages=199 |location=Bournemouth}}</ref> The 11-time [[pool (cue sports)|pool]] world champion,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.bca-pool.com/cgi/site/framegate.cgi?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.bca-pool.com%2Findustry%2Fhof%2Fhof.shtml&cat=I |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331115017/http://www.bca-pool.com/cgi/site/framegate.cgi?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.bca-pool.com%2Findustry%2Fhof%2Fhof.shtml&cat=I |website=bca-pool.com |publisher=Billiard Congress of America |title=The BCA Hall of Fame |archive-date=31 March 2007 |access-date=14 June 2021}}</ref> [[Jim Rempe]], made a {{cuegloss|break}} of 104 in defeating [[Martin Smith (snooker player)|Martin Smith]] 10–9.<ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Pool king hits a snooker century |work=Aberdeen Evening Express |date=28 March 1987 |page=11}}</ref> Veteran professional [[Bernard Bennett]] suffered the only [[whitewash]] of the first round, failing to win a frame against [[Billy Kelly (snooker player)|Billy Kelly]].<ref name="Ftsx0" />

In the second round, there were 32 matches. The youngest player in the competition, [[Stephen Hendry]], made a break of 108 during his 10–7 defeat of [[Mike Darrington]].<ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Highest break for Hendry |work=Dundee Courier |date=30 March 1987 |page=14}}</ref> Eight-times champion [[Fred Davis (snooker player)|Fred Davis]] lost 5–10 to [[Ken Owers]]. Another former champion, [[John Spencer (snooker player)|John Spencer]], who had won the title three times, eliminated [[Roger Bales]] 10–3.<ref name="QHvQx">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Sport In Brief: Davis loses – Snooker |work=The Times |location=London |date=1 April 1987 |page=42}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Tributes to cue king Fred Davis |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sport/79178.stm |work=BBC Sport |date=16 April 1998 |access-date=11 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110503211009/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/sport/79178.stm |archive-date=3 May 2011 |url-status=live}}</ref> Trailing after the first session of his qualifying match 3–5, [[Jimmy van Rensberg]] was taken to hospital with a suspected heart attack. However, he was later discharged and went on to win 10–6.<ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Regular Shorts |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |location=Australia |page=37 |date=2 April 1987}}</ref>

The third round consisted of 16 matches between winners from the second round.<ref name="Ftsx0" /> Hendry led 8–1 against Rempe at the end of their first session, with Rempe winning three consecutive frames in their second session before Hendry won the match 10–4. Gino Rigitano conceded the 11th frame of his match against [[Steve Newbury]] when there were still enough balls on the table for him to win, and when 4–9 down decided not to play the next frame, thereby losing 4–10.<ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Hendry takes a step nearer |work=Irish Independent |location=Sydney, Australia |page=14 |date=3 April 1987}}</ref> There were two 10–0 whitewashes: by [[Jon Wright (snooker player)|Jon Wright]] over [[Mark Wildman]] and by [[Tony Jones (snooker player)|Tony Jones]] over van Rensberg.<ref name="DOWNERQ" />

The fourth round also featured 16 matches, with 16 players seeded into the round each meeting one of the third round winners.<ref name="DOWNERQ" /> For the first time since turning professional, John Spencer failed to qualify for the event, as he was defeated 5–10 by [[Barry West]].<ref name="QHvQx" /> [[Bill Werbeniuk]] and [[Eddie Charlton]] also failed to qualify for the competition for the first time. Werbeniuk lost 8–10 to [[Mark Bennett (snooker player)|Mark Bennett]] and Charlton was defeated 4–10 by [[Warren King (snooker player)|Warren King]]. The only match to go to a {{cuegloss|deciding frame}} in round four was [[John Virgo]]'s 10–9 win over Tony Jones.<ref name="Ftsx0" /><ref>{{cite book |last1=Hayton |first1=Eric |last2=Dee |first2=John |date=2004 |title=The CueSport Book of Professional Snooker: The Complete Record & History |publisher=Rose Villa Publications |isbn=978-0-9548549-0-4 |pages=37–39}}</ref> [[Dene O'Kane]] scored five [[century break]]s across his three matches, a new record, including a 132.<ref name="z6zQf" /> He received £2,000 for this break, the highest during qualifying.<ref name="3Alg4" />

===First round===
[[File:Stephen Hendry PHC 2011.png|thumb|upright=0.85|alt=Stephen Hendry ready to play a shot on a red ball|[[Stephen Hendry]] (pictured in 2011) became the youngest player ever to win a match in the main stage of the tournament.]]
The first round of the main tournament was held from 18 to 23 April with matches played as the best-of-19 frames over two sessions.<ref>{{bulleted list|{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Regular Shorts |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |location=Australia |page=35 |date=20 April 1987}}|{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: Thorne suffers as Hendry makes history |work=The Times |location=London |date=17 April 1987 |page=40}}|{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: Knowles and Meo depart as the surprises continue |work=The Times |location=London |date=22 April 1987 |page=40}}|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Foulds revels in his freedom after Virgo opens the door |work=The Times |location=London |date=23 April 1987 |page=40}}|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Thorburn goes under to O'Kane |work=The Times |location=London |date=24 April 1987 |page=38}}}}</ref> Defending champion Joe Johnson played [[Eugene Hughes (snooker player)|Eugene Hughes]], with the match going to a deciding frame and Johnson winning 10–9.<ref name="cGIQM">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Regular Shorts |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |location=Australia |page=35 |date=20 April 1987}}</ref> Steve Davis was 7–1 ahead of Warren King at the end of their first session, but King then won six of the next seven frames to reduce Davis's lead to one frame at 8–7. Davis narrowly won frame 16 with a break of 63, then completed a 10–7 victory in frame 17, where he successfully gained enough {{cuegloss|foul}} points from King, despite {{cuegloss|snookers required|requiring snookers}}.<ref name="cGIQM" />

[[Murdo MacLeod]] defeated [[Rex Williams]] 10–5, despite Williams making the highest break of the first round, a 112.<ref name="pTykF">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: White hits back with top break |work=The Times |location=London |date=27 April 1987 |page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: Thorne's patience is tested |work=The Times |location=London |date=20 April 1987 |page=28}}</ref> The win made MacLeod the first Scottish player to secure a victory at the Crucible Theatre.<ref name="5cpi7">{{Cite news |title=1987: Davis' revenge on Johnson |work=BBC Sport |date=7 April 2004 |access-date=25 May 2020 |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/other_sports/snooker/3557639.stm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140517122042/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/other_sports/snooker/3557639.stm |archive-date=17 May 2014 |url-status=live }}</ref> Stephen Hendry met veteran player [[Willie Thorne]], led 5–4 at the end of their first session, and then took the first four frames in the second, before Thorne won three in a row. Hendry took the 17th frame to achieve a 10–7 win.<ref name="CwMMp">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: Thorne suffers as Hendry makes history |work=The Times |location=London |date=17 April 1987 |page=40}}</ref> At age 18 years and 97 days, Hendry became the youngest-ever player to win a World Championship match at the Crucible.<ref>{{cite book |last=Downer |first=Chris |title=Crucible Almanac |year=2012 |pages=132}}</ref> [[Steve Longworth]] led 5–4 after the first session of his match, and won five successive frames to defeat [[Kirk Stevens]] 10–5.<ref name="CwMMp" /> [[Terry Griffiths]] also progressed from a 5–4 interval lead to a 10–4 win, against [[Jim Wych]].<ref name="CwMMp" /> Alex Higgins, due to start a six-month ban after the Championship, eliminated first-year professional Jon Wright 10–6.<ref name="CwMMp" />

[[Jimmy White]] led [[Dean Reynolds]] 5–4 at the end of their first session. Reynolds won the first two frames of the second session both on {{cuegloss|re-spotted black|respotted blacks}}, before White went on to win 10–8.<ref name="GH97v">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: Knowles and Meo depart as the surprises continue |work=The Times |location=London |date=22 April 1987 |page=40}}</ref> From 7–8, White made breaks of 70, 75 and 59 to win the three frames he needed to progress.<ref name="EVERTONEMB"/> O'Kane, 39th in the rankings, won nine frames in a row against second-ranked player [[Cliff Thorburn]] to win 10–5.<ref name="alTkY" /><ref name="DC28APR" /> Thorburn's {{cuegloss|cue tip}} split during the sixth frame and was not replaced until after the end of the first session. He was unable to win a frame after the tip was broken.<ref name="SCENE">{{cite magazine |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Embassy world professional snooker championship |magazine=Snooker Scene |location=Halesowen |pages=5–19 |publisher=Everton's News Agency |date=June 1987}}</ref> Six-time champion [[Ray Reardon]] defeated debutant Barry West 10–5.<ref name="ruXQ6">{{cite web |title=Ray Reardon World Championship results |url=http://www.snookerdatabase.co.uk/PlayerDetails.aspx?PlayerKey=58&tourns=WC&plStat=2 |website=snookerdatabase.co.uk |publisher=Snooker Database |access-date=10 May 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305030324/http://www.snookerdatabase.co.uk/playerdetails.aspx?playerkey=58&plstat=2&tourns=wc#PlayerResults |archive-date=5 March 2016 |url-status=live}}</ref> Fourth seed [[Tony Knowles (snooker player)|Tony Knowles]] lost 6–10 to [[Mike Hallett]], having led 6–5, and 11th seed [[Tony Meo]] lost 8–10 to [[John Parrott]].<ref name="GH97v" />

[[Silvino Francisco]] and [[John Campbell (snooker player)|John Campbell]] played only eight of their scheduled nine frames in the first session, due to slow play, with Francisco leading 5–3 when they started the following session. Francisco won five consecutive frames at the start of the second session to complete a 10–3 win.<ref name="NVoEd">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Foulds revels in his freedom after Virgo opens the door |work=The Times |location=London |date=23 April 1987 |page=40}}</ref> [[Doug Mountjoy]] led [[David Taylor (snooker player)|David Taylor]] 6–3 after their first session, and won 10–6.<ref name="NVoEd" /> The [[1985 World Snooker Championship|1985]] champion [[Dennis Taylor]] led Bennett 8–1 before winning 10–4.<ref name="RVeAE">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Thorburn goes under to O'Kane |work=The Times |location=London |date=24 April 1987 |page=38}}</ref> Neal Foulds led John Virgo 7–2 after their first session and won 10–4.<ref name="NVoEd" />

===Second round===
The second round was played from 23 to 27 April with matches as the best-of-25 frames held over three sessions.<ref>{{bulleted list|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Thorburn goes under to O'Kane |work=The Times |location=London |date=24 April 1987 |page=38}}|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Davis on brink of victory |work=The Times |location=London |date=25 April 1987 |page=42}}|{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: White hits back with top break |work=The Times |location=London |date=27 April 1987 |page=36}}|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: White has to work for victory |work=The Times |location=London |date=28 April 1987 |page=40}}}}</ref> Johnson led MacLeod 6–2 and 10–6 after the first two sessions, winning 13–7.<ref name="neJCj">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Davis on brink of victory |work=The Times |location=London |date=25 April 1987 |page=42}}</ref> Hendry and Longworth were level at 4–4 after their first session, with Hendry then opening up a 7–4 lead with breaks of 96, 54 and 89, and finishing the second session 10–6 ahead after winning the 16th frame on a re-spotted black.<ref name="SCENE" /> Later trailing by six frames, Longworth won the 19th with a 103 break, before Hendry completed a 13–7 victory.<ref name="SCENE" /> Francisco and Hallett were at 8–8 after their second session before Hallett won three frames in a row, with breaks of 84, 47, 35 and 93. He also won the 20th frame, after requiring foul shots from Francisco, to lead 12–8, and clinched victory at 13–9 to reach his first World Championship quarter-final.<ref>{{cite news |last=Everton |first=Clive |date=28 April 1987 |title=Parrott brought to earth by White |work=The Guardian |location=London |page=32 }}</ref> Foulds led Taylor 5–3 after their first session,<ref name="WHB">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Snooker: White hits back with top break |work=The Times |location=London |date=27 April 1987 |page=36}}</ref> and won the match 13–10.<ref name="RGRwt" />

O'Kane eliminated another top-16 player with a victory over Mountjoy.<ref name="DC28APR">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Foulds puts Taylor out |work=Dundee Courier |page=12 |date=28 April 1987}}</ref> Mountjoy led 3–0, but O'Kane then won the next six frames, and won 13–5.<ref name="SCENE" /> White made a break of 114 in his match against Parrott, the highest in that year's championship to that point, and won 13–11. Five of the frames had been decided on the black, with White winning four of them.<ref name="pTykF" /><ref name="RGRwt">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Taylor is tamed |work=Irish Independent |date=28 April 1987 |page=14}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: White has to work for victory |work=The Times |location=London |date=28 April 1987 |page=40}}</ref> Higgins led Griffiths 6–3 after the first session but Griffiths won four frames in a row and the match was tied at 8–8. Griffiths went on to defeat Higgins 13–10.<ref name="pTykF" /><ref name="neJCj" /> Six-times winner of the event Reardon led 3–1 but lost 12 of the next 13 frames as he was eliminated 4–13 by Davis.<ref name="ruXQ6" /><ref name="RVeAE" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Barry West World Championship results |url=http://www.snookerdatabase.co.uk/PlayerDetails.aspx?PlayerKey=130&tourns=WC&plStat=2 |website=snookerdatabase.co.uk |publisher=Snooker Database |access-date=10 June 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305172422/http://www.snookerdatabase.co.uk/playerdetails.aspx?playerkey=130&plstat=2&tourns=wc#PlayerResults |archive-date=5 March 2016 |url-status=live}}</ref> After the tournament, Reardon dropped out of the top 16 in the end-of-season world rankings, moving down from 15 to 38.<ref>{{cite web |title=Ranking History |url=http://www.snooker.org/rnk/history.asp |website=snooker.org |publisher=Årdalen, Hermund |access-date=10 May 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181219070820/http://www.snooker.org/Rnk/history.asp |archive-date=19 December 2018 |url-status=live}}</ref>

===Quarter-finals===
[[File:German Masters 2015-Venue-Misc-12 (LezFraniak) - cropped to Neal Foulds.jpg|thumb|upright=0.8|[[Neal Foulds]] (pictured in 2015) reached the semi-finals for the only time in his career by defeating [[Mike Hallett]] 13–9.]]
<!--dates.-->The quarter-final matches were played over three sessions, in best-of-25 frames, on 28 and 29 April.<ref name="934FF">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: White fends off late charge |work=The Times |location=London |date=29 April 1987 |page=46}}</ref><ref name="PHou2">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Johnson is taken to brink |work=The Times |location=London |date=30 April 1987 |page=48}}</ref> Johnson led Hendry 8–1 and 12–8, before Hendry won four frames in a row to take the match to a deciding frame at 12–12. In the final frame, after Hendry failed to pot a {{cuegloss|red ball}}, Johnson made a break of 46 to take the frame and match 13–12.<ref name="alTkY" /><ref name="934FF" /><ref name="PHou2" /> Hallett compiled two century breaks in the first session of his match against Foulds, who also constructed a century break, with their first session ending at 4–4.<ref name="DEWbG">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Davis on a go slow |work=Newcastle Journal |date=29 April 1987 |page=13}}</ref> Foulds then opened up a lead, leading 10–6 and 12–7 before winning at 13–9.<ref name="zPOBX">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Davis, White confrontation in semi-final |work=Irish Independent |date=30 April 1987 |page=18}}</ref>

Davis was 4–3 ahead of Griffiths after their first session, and 10–5 ahead by the end of the second. He wrapped up a 13–5 win and with breaks of 62, 86 and 51 in three frames in the final session.<ref name="DEWbG" /><ref name="zPOBX" /> It was the fifth time that Davis had defeated Griffiths at the World Championship, and the largest winning margin of those encounters.<ref name="SCENE"/> White won all eight frames in the first session in his match against O'Kane, and after winning the first frame of the second session, led 9–0. O'Kane then won five consecutive frames, before White took four of the following five frames to win 13–6.<ref name="SCENE" /><ref name="934FF" /><ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Hendry's heartbreak |work=Aberdeen Press and Journal |date=30 April 1987 |page=28}}</ref>

===Semi-finals===
The semi-final matches were played as the best-of-31 frames, held over four sessions, from 30 April to 2 May.<ref>{{bulleted list|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Foulds battles his way out of trouble |work=The Times |location=London |date=1 May 1987 |page=42}}|{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Johnson is on song for final |work=The Times |location=London |date=2 May 1987 |page=44}}|{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Happy Johnson through as Davis defeats White |work=Sunday Independent |location=Dublin |date=3 May 1987 |page=27}}}}</ref> Johnson met Foulds in the first semi-final.<ref name="alTkY" /> The pair were tied at 3–3, but Foulds missed a pot on the {{cuegloss|black ball}} in frame seven allowing Johnson to take a one-frame lead after the first session.<ref name="T8gSJ">{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Seven-up for Neal and Joe |work=Liverpool Echo |date=1 May 1987 |page=62}}</ref> In frame eight, Foulds made a break of 48 to win the frame, and won frame nine, despite requiring foul shots. Johnson made breaks of 47 in each of the next two frames to lead 6–5.<ref name="X9gGm">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Johnson is on song for final |work=The Times |location=London |date=2 May 1987 |page=44}}</ref> Foulds took the next before Johnson, with his fourth break of 47 in four frames, took the lead again. Foulds won the last frame of the session with a break of 45 to leave them all square at 7–7 after two sessions, and made a break of 114 in the 15th frame to go one ahead before Johnson won seven frames in a row to lead 14–8.<ref name="T8gSJ" /><ref name="X9gGm" /><ref name="0dudz">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Foulds battles his way out of trouble |work=The Times |location=London |date=1 May 1987 |page=42}}</ref> After this, Foulds won frame 23, but Johnson won the next two frames to win the match 16–9 to reach his second final.<ref name="5cpi7" />

Davis and White had been level at 4–4 after their first session, with Davis winning the first four of their second session to lead 8–4 and finishing that session 9–6 ahead.<ref name="X9gGm" /><ref name="0dudz" /> In the first frame of the third session, White was on course to make a [[maximum break]], having potted ten reds and nine blacks, but missed the tenth black. After this, Davis required White to make foul shots in order to gain the necessary penalty points from them for Davis to win the frame. Aided by a {{cuegloss|fluke|fluked}} black, and by a {{cuegloss|free ball}} following a foul by White, Davis eventually won the frame by one point. John Hennessey in ''Pot Black'' magazine wrote that "at that moment White lost the chance of claiming his first world title," adding that White's father later said that losing the frame affected White badly during the following three.<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Hennessey |first=John |date=June 1987 |title=And all the king's men help put Davis together again |magazine=Pot Black |location=London |publisher=Pot Black Publishing |page=6}}</ref> However, White later compiled a 119 break, the new highest in the competition, overtaking his earlier 114 in the second round, and ended the third session 9–13 behind.<ref>{{cite news |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=Happy Johnson through as Davis defeats White |work=Sunday Independent |location=Dublin |date=3 May 1987 |page=27}}</ref> White took the first frame of the fourth session, but lost three of the following four frame as Davis won the match 16–11, concluding with a 74 break in the 27th frame.<ref name="SCENE" />

===Final===
[[File:Steve Davis at Sports Club Turku, Finland.jpg|thumb|alt=Steve Davis about to play a shot|[[Steve Davis]] (pictured in 2008) won his fourth world title, defeating [[Joe Johnson (snooker player)|Joe Johnson]] 18–14.]]
The final was played as a best-of-35 frames match held over four sessions between Davis and Johnson on 3 and 4 May 1987.<ref name="tV7DU">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Johnson stages a comeback to put Davis under threat |work=The Times |location=London |date=4 May 1987 |page=28}}</ref><ref name="vA2ml">{{cite news |last=Friskin |first=Sydney |title=Snooker: Davis avenges defeat and regains the title |work=The Times |location=London |date=5 May 1987 |page=40}}</ref> It was the first time that the same two players had met in the final at the Crucible for the second year in a row.<ref name="fVuRu">{{cite web |last=Turner |first=Chris |title=A brief history of the World Professional Championship |url=http://www.cajt.pwp.blueyonder.co.uk/world.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130416080933/http://www.cajt.pwp.blueyonder.co.uk/world.html |archive-date=16 April 2013 |website=cajt.pwp.blueyonder.co.uk |publisher=Chris Turner's Snooker Archive |year=2008 |access-date=24 February 2011}}</ref> The last time that two players had met in consecutive finals at the World Championship was when Fred Davis and [[John Pulman]] had both reached the final in [[1955 World Professional Match-play Championship|1955]] and [[1956 World Professional Match-play Championship|1956]], played at the [[Blackpool Tower#Tower Circus|Tower Circus]], Blackpool.<ref name="fVuRu" /> The next time it would happen was when Hendry and White met in three consecutive finals between [[1992 World Snooker Championship|1992]] and [[1994 World Snooker Championship|1994]].<ref name="fVuRu" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Hall of Fame |url=http://www.snooker.org/Trn/hof.asp?pEvent=World |website=snooker.org |publisher=Årdalen, Hermund  |access-date=24 February 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190728220548/http://www.snooker.org/trn/hof.asp?pEvent=World |archive-date=28 July 2019 |url-status=live}}</ref> This was also the first time that the final had been contested by the top two seeds of the tournament.<ref>{{cite book |last=Downer |first=Chris |title=Crucible Almanac |year=2012 |pages=121}}</ref> The final was refereed by [[Len Ganley]], the second time he had taken charge of the World Championship final.<ref name="wst._Trip">{{Cite web |title=Triple Crown Final Referees |website=wst.tv |publisher=World Snooker |access-date=5 April 2021 |url= https://wst.tv/corporate/referees/triple-crown-final-referees/ |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200111143925/https://wst.tv/corporate/referees/triple-crown-final-referees/ |url-status= dead |archive-date= 11 January 2020 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Downer |first=Chris |title=Crucible Almanac |year=2012 |pages=143}}</ref>

In frame one, Davis compiled a 127 break, which remained the highest break of that year's tournament.<ref name="K2yy5" /> Johnson responded winning three frames in a row followed by Davis taking two to level at 3-3. Johnson was 4–3 ahead at the end of the first session.<ref name="SCENE" /> Davis added three successive frames to lead 6–4 at the start of the second session, then Johnson won the 11th frame with a break of 101 before falling three behind as Davis won the next two frames.<ref name="SCENE" /> Johnson was behind in the 14th frame but won it with a break of 73. The last two frames of the session were both won with the aid of fluked reds: the 15th frame by Davis, and the last of the day by Johnson, the day finishing with Davis leading 9–7.<ref name="alTkY" /><ref name="tV7DU" /> On the second day, Johnson won the first frame of the third session to reduce Davis's lead to one frame. Davis then took four consecutive frames to lead 13–8, but missed the last red ball when on a break of 52. Johnson then cleared up to the black, which Davis would have required to level the scores in the frame. However, Davis left the black in a position that it could be potted from, and Johnson won the frame.<ref name="tV7DU" /> Davis won the next frame, to lead 14–9 at the end of the third session.<ref name="alTkY" />

Johnson made a break of 52 in the first frame of the fourth session, but failed on an attempt to pot a red, which gave Davis an opportunity. Davis then made a break of 35, but left an easy {{cuegloss|green ball}} for Johnson, who cleared to the {{cuegloss|pink ball}} to win. In the next frame, Johnson made a break of 62, and then Davis attempted a {{cuegloss|clearance}}, but missed the {{cuegloss|yellow ball}}. Johnson potted the yellow from distance and cleared to the blue, with Davis then conceding the frame. Johnson led 50–0 in the next frame, and with both players making a number of errors Davis left him an easy brown that allowed Johnson in to win his fourth consecutive frame to move to one behind at 13–14.<ref name="alTkY" /><ref name="5cpi7" /> Davis had breaks of 64 and 40 to lead 15–13, a break of 73 to help make it 16–13, and 17–13 winning frame 30, decided on the final {{cuegloss|colours}}. Johnson won another frame, with a break of 67, before Davis clinched victory with a break of 73 to make it 18–14, achieving his fourth World Championship title.<ref name="alTkY" /><ref name="tV7DU" /><ref name="vA2ml" /> After the match, Davis said: "Winning this is better than 1981 because I've experienced getting beat in the final and its horrible."<ref name="EVERTONEMB">{{cite book |last=Everton |first=Clive |date=1993 |title=The Embassy Book of World Snooker |publisher=Bloomsbury |location=London |isbn=0-7475-1610-3 |pages=116–119}}</ref>

==Aftermath==
During the tournament, there were reports that Foulds and WPBSA chairman Williams were taking [[beta blocker]]s. These were banned under [[International Olympic Committee]] rules, but not prohibited in snooker.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Goodbody |first1=John |last2=Friskin |first2=Sydney |title=Snooker player, 23, admits using heart drug |work=The Times |location=London |date=24 April 1987 |page=1}}</ref> [[Colin Moynihan, 4th Baron Moynihan|Colin Moynihan]], a [[Member of parliament|Member of the British Parliament]], called for Williams to resign and any players using beta blockers to withdraw from competing.<ref>{{cite news |last=Goodbody |first=John |title=MP in call for Williams to resign his post |work=The Times |location=London |date=27 April 1987 |page=36}}</ref> In November 1987, the WPBSA was suspended from using the Sports Council's drug-testing facilities until the use of beta blockers was banned from the sport. Moynihan wrote to Williams supporting the ban that had been proposed by the Sports Council's Drug Advisory Group.<ref>{{cite news |last=Goodbody |first=John |title=snooker pays penalty for failing to act |work=The Times |location=London |date=25 November 1987 |page=48}}</ref> Williams resigned as WPBSA chairman in November 1987, having received criticism over the drug testing issue as well as over his personal business connections with promoters [[Barry Hearn]] and [[Frank Warren (promoter)|Frank Warren]].<ref>{{cite news |last=Acteson |first=Steve |title=Snooker: Williams makes the break |work=The Times |location=London |date=30 November 1987 |page=40 }}</ref> The WPBSA voted to ban the use of beta blockers, other than [[cardioselective beta-blockers|cardioselective types]], in January 1988, with the ban to come into effect from the start of the [[1988–89 snooker season]].<ref>{{cite news |last=Acteson |first=Steve |title=Beta-blocker ban may mean the end for Williams |work=The Times |location=London |date=8 January 1988 |page=32}}</ref>

==Century breaks==
There were 18 century breaks at the championship. The highest was a 127 made by Steve Davis in the first frame of the final.<ref name="K2yy5" /> This was the lowest world championship high break since the event moved to the Crucible Theatre in 1977. The highest break in 1977 was 135 by John Spencer, which was the lowest to be the highest break at the world championship until 1986, when Davis's 134 was the highest.<ref>{{cite web |title=Crucible Centuries |url=http://www.snooker.org/Plr/wc_centuries.shtml |website=snooker.org |publisher=Årdalen, Hermund  |access-date=2 May 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110523045039/http://www.snooker.org/plr/wc_centuries.shtml |archive-date=23 May 2011 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Kastner |first=Hugo |title=Snooker – Spieler, Regeln & Rekorde |date=May 2011 |url=http://www.hugo-kastner.at/snooker_crucible_records_mai11.pdf |website=hugo-kastner.at |access-date=27 May 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203014834/http://www.hugo-kastner.at/snooker_crucible_records_mai11.pdf |archive-date=3 December 2013 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="DOWNERB">{{cite book|last=Downer|first=Chris|title=Crucible Almanac |chapter=The breaks: Crucible centuries by year|year=2019|page=162 |location=Bournemouth}}</ref>
